summary: Cross-chapter audit of 18-chapter manuscript (~76,800 prose words, 2,515 prose lines, ~6,040 sentences). REDUNDANCY: Found 10 character re-introduction defects (Aldric 'report/order' formula used 8x across 6 chapters; Derry compulsive-talking trait re-explained 6x; Derry 'farmer's face/hands' re-bundled 3x in 140 lines of Ch13; 'Maren's body was already moving' repeated verbatim Ch4→Ch5). Found proof-of-baseline over-accumulation for Maren's fraud identity (~65 hits across 17/18 chapters), 'eleven years' phrase (30 hits, 23 in first 4 chapters), and Derry's family motivation (~75 hits, 16 in Ch13 alone). Em-dash density is 16.1% manuscript-wide (404/2515 prose lines), with 11 chapters exceeding 10% and 7 chapters exceeding 25%. 'Something' appears 286 times (2.98 per 800 words, 3x the 1-per-800 threshold), affecting all 18 chapters. Sentence-opener monotony: 'He' at 5.8% (below 7% threshold manuscript-wide), no finding emitted. CONSISTENCY: Found 4 genuine contradictions — bowl chip has 3 mutually exclusive origin stories (Ch1/Ch2/Ch12), Maren's posture-learning method contradicts across chapters (woodcut Ch1 vs watching Ch3/Ch11, with conflicting locations), ritual count jumps from 16 (Ch3) to 130 (Ch11) without intervening rituals, and bowl color changes from brown-glazed to white (Ch2→Ch17).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2 L588: 'the voice of a man filing a report to an order that no longer existed'; Ch4 L878: 'delivered in the cadence of a man filing reports to an order that no longer existed.' Also Ch4 L878: 'Two days of Derry talking about anything that wasn't the bounty' re-explains Derry's compulsive-talking trait established at Ch3 L625.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch4 opening re-introduces both Aldric and Derry using formulas established 1-2 chapters earlier. The Aldric 'report to an order that no longer existed' formula (first used Ch2 L588) is repeated nearly verbatim. Derry's nervous-talking trait (established Ch3 L625) is re-explained editorially.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or substantially vary the 'filing reports to an order that no longer existed' formula at L878. Replace with a fresh Aldric-voice observation. For Derry's talking, show the behavior without re-explaining its meaning — trust the reader's Ch3 memory.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4 opening contains no variant of 'report(s) to an order that no longer existed' and does not editorially explain that Derry talks when nervous.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2 L588: 'filing a report to an order that no longer existed'; Ch4 L878: 'filing reports to an order that no longer existed'; Ch5 L1255: 'every word set down with the precision of a man filing a report for an audience that no longer existed.' Three consecutive chapter-openings (Ch2, Ch4, Ch5) use variants of the same 12-word formula.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch5 L1255 is the third use of 'filing a report for an order/audience that no longer existed' in four chapters. This is a near-verbatim cross-chapter repetition (12 content words with minor substitution). The adjacent Ch4→Ch5 repetition is the most jarring.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Remove the 'report/audience that no longer existed' formula entirely from Ch5. Characterize Aldric's voice through how it sounds in this specific tactical moment — e.g., through the shift from formality to urgency, not through the now-worn formula.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5 contains zero variants of 'filing a report' or 'order/audience that no longer existed' as an Aldric descriptor.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L994: 'But Maren's body was already moving.' Ch5 L1281: 'Aldric's assessment dissolved into irrelevance because Maren's body was already moving.' Identical 6-word core sentence in adjacent chapters, both marking reflex overriding deliberation.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'Maren's body was already moving' appears verbatim at Ch4 L994 and Ch5 L1281, one chapter apart. Both serve the same narrative function (body acts before mind decides).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Vary Ch5 L1281. Convey the reflex-override through a different construction — e.g., focus on a specific body part (the hand snapping to the knife already does this at L1283) and cut the general 'body was already moving' sentence.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5 does not contain 'Maren's body was already moving' or a near-identical variant.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L625: establishes Derry's compulsive-talking; Ch4 L888/902: re-explains with strap-pulling; Ch8 L1968: 'He'd been adjusting the straps since they'd left the outskirts of Silt — pulling at the leather, letting it go, pulling again — and Maren recognized the gesture. It was the thing Derry did when he wanted to talk but hadn't decided what to say yet. The strap adjustment was a placeholder for conversation.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 L1968 re-explains Derry's strap-pulling as a conversational placeholder with a three-sentence editorial gloss. This dynamic was established in Ch3-4 and does not need re-teaching by Ch8.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Show Derry adjusting straps without the editorial explanation. Cut 'and Maren recognized the gesture. It was the thing Derry did when he wanted to talk but hadn't decided what to say yet. The strap adjustment was a placeholder for conversation.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 strap-pulling passage shows the behavior without narratorial gloss explaining its meaning.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L625: 'the worse things got, the more Derry talked about his garden, his daughter'; Ch10 L2562: 'The Derry who filled every silence with stories about Pell's counting lessons and Lenna's tomato plants was walking beside Maren now like a man saving his words.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch10 L2562 re-summarizes Derry's compulsive-talking-about-family trait for the 4th time (after Ch3 L625, Ch4 L878, Ch8 L1968). The trait is thoroughly established by now.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Show Derry's current silence directly — e.g., 'Derry offered dried meat at dawn, asked if the road felt right, and let a comment about clouds die.' The contrast with his normal behavior is implicit to any reader who has reached Ch10.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 opening does not summarize Derry's habit of filling silences with domestic stories.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch10 L2562: 'The Derry who filled every silence with stories about Pell's counting lessons and Lenna's tomato plants'; Ch11 L2821: 'Derry talked about his daughter the way most people breathed — because it was necessary, because it happened without effort, because stopping would require a conscious decision that something had fundamentally changed.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch11 L2821 re-explains Derry's Pell-talk habit one chapter after Ch10 L2562 did the same. This is the 5th re-explanation of a trait first established in Ch3 L625. The Ch10→Ch11 adjacent pair is the most jarring instance.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the simile at L2821. Let 'Pell hadn't come up since the night before Caul found them' carry the weight alone — the reader knows what Pell's absence means by now.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 does not editorially explain Derry's Pell-talk habit. The absence of Pell's name is noted without a characterization gloss.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13 L3567: 'The open, friendly face that had made him such a good partner in the fraud — that trustworthy farmer's face'; Ch13 L3651 (84 lines later): 'Stocky, solid Derry with his farmer's hands and his open face'; Ch13 L3705 (54 lines after that): 'Heavy, rough, the hand of a man who knew soil and livestock and the specific weight of holding a child while she slept.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 re-bundles Derry's established descriptor package three times in 140 lines, using 'farmer's face/hands' and 'open face' as labels. L3651 reads as a character-sheet re-introduction. These descriptors were already established in Ch6 L1518, Ch7 L1679, Ch10 L2658.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep L3705 (the strongest, with its emotional specificity about holding a sleeping child). Cut or substantially vary L3567's 'trustworthy farmer's face' and L3651's 'Stocky, solid Derry with his farmer's hands and his open face.' Replace with details specific to this departure moment.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 contains at most one passage labeling Derry with 'farmer's hands/face' or 'open face.' No passage re-bundles three or more established descriptors into a single sentence.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Aldric 'report/order' formula instances: Ch2 L588, Ch4 L878, Ch5 L1255, Ch8 L2108, Ch8 L2168, Ch9 L2257, Ch14 L3754: 'precision as habit, the ghost of field reports filed to an order that no longer existed.' This is the 7th variant.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch14 L3754 uses the 'reports filed to an order that no longer existed' formula for the 7th time. By Ch14, Aldric's voice is thoroughly established across 12 prior chapters.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the 'reports/order that no longer existed' formula from Ch14. If Aldric's formality needs noting, use a fresh construction reflecting how Maren's relationship with Aldric has evolved by this point in the novel.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 does not contain 'report(s)' or 'order that no longer existed' as an Aldric voice descriptor.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8 L2108: 'The voice of a man filing a report he didn't want to file'; Ch8 L2168: 'the specific precision of a man who had trained himself never to flinch from a report.' Two 'report' variants within the same chapter (instances 4 and 5 of 8 total).
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 doubles up on the 'report' metaphor for Aldric's speech within a single chapter (L2108 and L2168), compounding the novel-wide overuse.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut L2168's 'report' reference. Keep L2108 ('report he didn't want to file') as the chapter's single use since it is more emotionally specific.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 contains at most one use of 'report' as an Aldric speech metaphor.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1789: 'clean hands and a ceramic bowl that had never held anything real'; Ch14 L3992: 'No staining. No ritual wear. Clean, cheap, useless'; Ch14 L4040 (48 lines later): 'A prop. A fake thing in a real place.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch14 describes Maren's bowl as fraudulent twice within 48 lines (L3992 and L4040). Both echo the same characterization from Ch7 L1789.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep L3992. Cut or rework L4040's 'A prop. A fake thing in a real place' — the bowl's fraudulence was established 48 lines earlier and does not need re-stating.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 does not call the bowl fraudulent/fake/prop more than once within 50 lines.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 contains 4 uses of 'eleven years' at L1751, L1769, L1861, L1941. The phrase has already appeared 23 times in Ch1-Ch4. Manuscript-wide total: 30 occurrences across 13 chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: The 'eleven years' duration marker is the most repeated factual anchor in the novel (30 occurrences). By Ch7, the reader has encountered it 23 times. Ch7 uses it 4 more times in a single chapter — including twice within 80 lines at L1861 and L1941 — without new information or leverage.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch7's 'eleven years' usage from 4 to at most 2. Keep the instances that carry dialogue weight (L1769 in Thessa's confrontation is the strongest). Cut L1941's instance, as the chapter has already made this point three times.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch7 contains at most 2 instances of the 'eleven years' phrase.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 L2857: 'the way he'd arranged it a hundred and thirty times before, the familiar choreography of the fake ritual'; Ch11 L2887: 'The fake ritual was performance. That was all it had ever been. At sixteen, he'd learned the postures from watching a real sin-eater...'; Ch11 L2907: 'instead of murmuring the fake incantation.' Ch10 also contains 6 fraud re-statements at L2620-L2764. Together Ch10-Ch11 re-prove fraud 11 times in adjacent chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: By Ch11, Maren's fraud identity has been proved ~45 times across prior chapters. Ch11 adds 5 more re-statements (L2857, L2887, L2907, L2905, L3033) in a chapter where Maren is actively trying to do real sin-eating — the fraud baseline is being re-proved while the character is moving beyond it. Adjacent to Ch10's 6 fraud re-statements.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 2-3 of Ch11's fraud re-statements. The act of attempting genuine consumption already implies the contrast with his fake past. L2887's origin-story passage (postures from watching) could be cut entirely since the reader learned this in Ch1/Ch3. Keep L2857 if it serves the ritual scene.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 contains at most 2 uses of 'fake/fraud/faking/sham' or equivalent fraud re-statements. No passage in Ch11 re-tells Maren's fraud origin story.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13 contains 16 Derry-family references including L3581 (Pell turning three, Lenna needing help, Hest), L3609 (Pell's counting), L3689 (chickens), L3695 (herb box). These repeat specific domestic details used in Ch6 (L1508-1582: Pell/Lenna cluster of 10) and Ch9 (L2347: Pell's arithmetic). Manuscript-wide total: ~75 Derry-family-motivation hits.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 (Derry's departure) contains 16 re-statements of Derry's family motivation, repeating specific domestic details (counting lessons, tomato plants, chickens, herb box) already used in Ch6 and Ch9. The emotional weight is earned but the specific details are recycled rather than fresh.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce from 16 to ~8-10 family references. Keep the most emotionally specific moments (L3581's 'She's turning three while we're on the road' is the strongest). Cut or vary repeated details: Pell's counting was used in Ch6 and Ch9 — choose a new domestic detail or let silence carry the weight.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 does not repeat more than one domestic detail (counting, tomatoes, chickens, herb box) that was used in Ch6 or Ch9. Total family-motivation references are reduced to 10 or fewer.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch6 L1508, L1512, L1516, L1518, L1520, L1522, L1574, L1578, L1582, L1636: 10 Derry-family-motivation references in a 158-line chapter. Lines 1508-1522 contain 5 references in 14 lines.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch6 clusters 10 Derry-family-motivation references in a chapter of only 158 prose lines (6.3% density). Lines 1508-1522 stack 5 references in 14 lines. After Ch3's 8 family references, the baseline is already established.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce from 10 to ~5-6 family references. The cluster at L1508-1522 can be thinned — keep the strongest 2-3 and let the quiet chapter texture do the emotional work without hammering.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch6 contains no more than 6 Derry-family references. No 15-line span contains more than 3.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 contains 29 uses of 'something' in 3,911 prose words (5.93 per 800 words). Manuscript-wide: 286 total occurrences across 76,797 prose words (2.98 per 800 words vs. threshold of 1.0 per 800). Ch11 is the worst single-chapter offender at 6x threshold.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'Something' appears 286 times manuscript-wide (3x the 1-per-800-word threshold). Ch11 alone has 29 instances in 3,911 words — nearly 6x the threshold. This creates a pervasive hedging quality where specificity would serve better.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch11's 'something' count from 29 to at most 5. Replace vague 'something' constructions with specific nouns, sensations, or actions. E.g., 'something inside him shifted' → name what shifted; 'like something being dissolved' → name the dissolution.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 contains at most 5 instances of the word 'something.' Each remaining instance is genuinely vague by narrative intent, not by prose habit.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch16 contains 21 uses of 'something' in 4,114 prose words (4.08 per 800 words). Manuscript-wide: 286/76,797 = 2.98 per 800 words.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch16 has 21 'something' instances in 4,114 words — 4x the threshold. This is the second-worst chapter after Ch11.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch16's 'something' count from 21 to at most 5. The Vess separation chapter benefits from specificity about what is being lost, felt, or released.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 contains at most 5 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8 contains 16 uses of 'something' in 3,346 prose words (3.83 per 800 words). Manuscript-wide: 286/76,797 = 2.98 per 800 words.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 has 16 'something' instances in 3,346 words — nearly 4x threshold. The Vess backstory chapter would benefit from replacing vague references with specific detail about what she remembers and doesn't.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch8's 'something' count from 16 to at most 4. Replace hedges with concrete details or honest acknowledgment of gaps (Vess's missing memories can be named as missing rather than 'something').
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 contains at most 4 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch10 contains 19 uses of 'something' in 4,603 prose words (3.30 per 800 words). Manuscript-wide: 286/76,797 = 2.98 per 800 words.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch10 has 19 'something' instances in 4,603 words — 3.3x threshold.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch10's 'something' count from 19 to at most 6. The Caul confrontation chapter should name what Maren feels, fears, and recognizes rather than hedging.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 contains at most 6 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13 contains 16 uses of 'something' in 3,509 prose words (3.65 per 800 words). Manuscript-wide: 286/76,797 = 2.98 per 800 words.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 has 16 'something' instances in 3,509 words — 3.6x threshold.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch13's 'something' count from 16 to at most 4. Derry's departure is emotionally specific — the prose should match.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 contains at most 4 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch16: 45 em-dash-containing prose lines / 126 total prose lines = 35.7%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%. Target: bring manuscript below 8%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch16 has the highest em-dash density in the manuscript at 35.7% (45/126 prose lines). More than 1 in 3 prose lines contains an em-dash. Manuscript-wide density is 16.1%, double the 8% target.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 45 to at most 10 (targeting ~8%). Convert parenthetical em-dash pairs to commas, colons, or sentence breaks. Reserve em-dashes for genuine interruptions and sharp pivots.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 10 out of ~126 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch6: 26 em-dash-containing prose lines / 77 total prose lines = 33.8%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch6 has 33.8% em-dash density (26/77 prose lines) — the second highest rate. In a chapter of only 77 prose lines, 26 contain em-dashes.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 26 to at most 6 (targeting ~8%). Prioritize cutting em-dash pairs used as parentheticals and replacing with commas or restructured sentences.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch6 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 6 out of ~77 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2: 43 em-dash-containing prose lines / 130 total prose lines = 33.1%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch2 has 33.1% em-dash density (43/130 prose lines). This is the reader's second chapter — high em-dash density here sets an unfortunate stylistic expectation.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 43 to at most 10 (targeting ~8%). Prioritize Aldric's dialogue lines, which likely account for many em-dashes as interjections.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 10 out of ~130 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11: 34 em-dash-containing prose lines / 117 total prose lines = 29.1%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch11 has 29.1% em-dash density (34/117 prose lines).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 34 to at most 9 (targeting ~8%).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 9 out of ~117 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch14: 49 em-dash-containing prose lines / 177 total prose lines = 27.7%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch14 has the highest absolute count of em-dash lines (49) at 27.7% density.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 49 to at most 14 (targeting ~8%). The Crook arrival chapter has many character voices — restructure multi-clause em-dash sentences into separate sentences.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 14 out of ~177 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8: 34 em-dash-containing prose lines / 124 total prose lines = 27.4%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 has 27.4% em-dash density (34/124 prose lines).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 34 to at most 10 (targeting ~8%).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 10 out of ~124 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13: 37 em-dash-containing prose lines / 143 total prose lines = 25.9%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 has 25.9% em-dash density (37/143 prose lines).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 37 to at most 11 (targeting ~8%).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 11 out of ~143 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch18: 29 em-dash-containing prose lines / 118 total prose lines = 24.6%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch18 has 24.6% em-dash density (29/118 prose lines). As the final chapter, this density compounds the manuscript-wide pattern.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 29 to at most 9 (targeting ~8%).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch18 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 9 out of ~118 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3: 27 em-dash-containing prose lines / 126 total prose lines = 21.4%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch3 has 21.4% em-dash density (27/126 prose lines).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 27 to at most 10 (targeting ~8%).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 10 out of ~126 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7: 33 em-dash-containing prose lines / 157 total prose lines = 21.0%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch7 has 21.0% em-dash density (33/157 prose lines).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 33 to at most 13 (targeting ~8%).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch7 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 13 out of ~157 prose lines (≤8%).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9: 17 em-dash-containing prose lines / 168 total prose lines = 10.1%. Manuscript-wide: 404/2515 = 16.1%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch9 is at 10.1% em-dash density (17/168 prose lines) — just above the 10% threshold.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines from 17 to at most 13 (targeting ~8%). Lower priority than chapters above 20%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9 em-dash-containing prose lines are at most 13 out of ~168 prose lines (≤8%).

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 L9: 'He'd chipped the rim twice and glued it back both times with pine resin'; Ch2 L424: 'a chip on the rim from five years ago when he'd dropped it packing up after the Halversen ritual'; Ch12 L3328: 'a small chip at the rim where he'd knocked it against a cart wheel in Fenhold three summers ago.' Three mutually exclusive backstories for the bowl's chip(s).
consistency_findings.problem: The bowl chip has three contradictory origins. Ch1 says two chips, both repaired with pine resin. Ch2 says one chip from dropping it after the Halversen ritual five years ago. Ch12 says one chip from hitting a cart wheel three summers ago. These cannot all be true.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Unify the chip backstory with Ch1 L9 as canonical (two chips, pine resin). Revise Ch2 L424 to match — either reference one of the two chips consistent with Ch1's account, or remove the specific origin story and keep only the physical description.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2's chip description does not contradict Ch1's account of two chips repaired with pine resin. The origin event (if given) is consistent across all chapters.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 L9: 'He'd chipped the rim twice and glued it back both times with pine resin'; Ch12 L3328: 'a small chip at the rim where he'd knocked it against a cart wheel in Fenhold three summers ago.'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch12 provides a third incompatible origin for the bowl chip (cart wheel, three summers ago), contradicting both Ch1 (two chips, pine resin) and Ch2 (Halversen ritual, five years ago).
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Revise Ch12 L3328 to match the canonical chip description from Ch1. Remove the 'cart wheel in Fenhold three summers ago' origin or align it with Ch1's two-chip, pine-resin account.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12's chip reference is consistent with Ch1 L9's description.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 L19: 'He'd copied it from a woodcut in a book he'd stolen from a traveling merchant when he was sixteen'; Ch3 L825: 'the words he'd memorized from watching a real sin-eater work once, at a distance, in a village that wasn't Fenhold, when he was sixteen'; Ch11 L2887: 'At sixteen, he'd learned the postures from watching a real sin-eater work a field outside Fenhold — an old woman whose name he'd never learned.'
consistency_findings.problem: Three incompatible origins for how Maren learned sin-eater postures. Ch1 says from a stolen book's woodcut. Ch3 and Ch11 say from watching a real sin-eater — but Ch3 says 'in a village that wasn't Fenhold' while Ch11 says 'outside Fenhold.' Each passage frames its version as THE origin of his technique.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Establish one canonical learning method and location. The woodcut (Ch1) and the observation (Ch3/Ch11) could coexist (woodcut for postures, observation for rhythm) if explicitly reconciled. But the location must be unified: either 'outside Fenhold' or 'a village that wasn't Fenhold,' not both. Revise Ch11 L2887 to match whichever origin Ch1 and Ch3 agree on.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11's description of how Maren learned the postures does not contradict Ch1 or Ch3 in either method or location.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 L19: 'He'd copied it from a woodcut in a book he'd stolen'; Ch3 L825: 'the words he'd memorized from watching a real sin-eater work once, at a distance, in a village that wasn't Fenhold.'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch3 attributes Maren's ritual knowledge to watching a real sin-eater, contradicting Ch1's woodcut origin. Ch3 also specifies 'a village that wasn't Fenhold,' which Ch11 later contradicts with 'outside Fenhold.'
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Revise Ch3 L825 to be compatible with Ch1's woodcut origin, or explicitly distinguish what was learned from the woodcut (postures) vs. what was learned from observation (pacing, murmuring). The location should match Ch11 if Ch11's account is kept.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3's account of how Maren learned the ritual does not contradict Ch1's woodcut account.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch3 L825: 'He'd held it over sixteen bodies'; Ch11 L2857: 'the way he'd arranged it a hundred and thirty times before.' The narrative spans roughly 2-3 weeks; no additional fake rituals are performed between Ch3 and Ch11.
consistency_findings.problem: Ch3 claims Maren has performed the ritual over only 16 bodies. Ch11 claims 130 times. Over eleven years of regular work, 130 is plausible; 16 is implausibly low. No intervening rituals occur between these chapters to account for the difference.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Revise Ch3 L825 to replace 'sixteen bodies' with a number consistent with Ch11's 130 (or a number close to it). Alternatively, if 'sixteen' refers only to death rituals and '130' includes non-death clearances, make that distinction explicit.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The ritual count in Ch3 is consistent with the count in Ch11 and with eleven years of regular practice.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch2 L424: 'Ordinary ceramic, brown-glazed'; Ch16 L4542: 'the color somewhere between mud and dried blood, an indeterminate brown'; Ch17 L4735: 'The ceramic was white and cracked.'
consistency_findings.problem: The bowl is consistently described as brown-glazed throughout the novel (Ch2 L424, Ch12 L3328 'cheap ceramic,' Ch16 L4542 'indeterminate brown'). At Ch17 L4735, it is suddenly 'white.' Even if the crack from Ch15 exposed pale clay underneath, calling the entire bowl 'white' contradicts every prior description.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Revise Ch17 L4735 to describe the bowl as brown (or cracked with pale clay showing at the break) rather than 'white.' The crack can expose lighter material without changing the overall color.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17's bowl description is consistent with the established brown-glazed color. If the crack exposes different material, the overall bowl is still described as brown.
